notes: Reverse migration from NSubstitute to Moq. This direction introduces indirection (Mock<T> wrapper, .Object accessor) that does not exist in NSubstitute. Several NSubstitute patterns have no clean Moq equivalent.

mappings.notes: Moq returns Mock<T> wrapper, not T directly. All usages of the variable must be updated: pass mock.Object where T is expected, and use mock.Setup/Verify for configuration.
Moq requires wrapping in Setup lambda expression. Argument matchers must also be translated.

mappings.target: // Moq: use Callback() on Setup -- structural rewrite required
mappings.notes: NSubstitute's Arg.Do captures argument inline; Moq requires .Callback() chained on Setup. No direct translation.
Must replace each parameter with It.IsAny<T>(). No single Moq equivalent to ReceivedWithAnyArgs.
Moq Returns also accepts Func<TResult> but the CallInfo parameter must be restructured or dropped.

asymmetries.issue: NSubstitute's substitute IS the interface. Moq wraps it in Mock<T> requiring .Object access everywhere the interface is passed. This is a pervasive structural change.
No Moq equivalent for inline argument capture. Must restructure to Setup().Callback().
Moq has no single-call equivalent. Must enumerate all parameters with It.IsAny<T>().
NSubstitute's When().Do() pattern maps to Moq's Setup().Callback(), but the syntax is significantly different.
